title: The letter behind the plaster
summary: During the renovation of a flat in the Staré Mesto district of Bratislava, a paper conservator named Vlasta Dudášová is called in to assess a folded letter that the builders found inside the wall. The letter is in Slovak, dated 1947, and addressed to no one in particular. The decision about what to do with it turns out to be more difficult than she had expected.
body:
Vlasta Dudášová was called to the flat on Heydukova Street on a Tuesday morning in November. A builder, knocking out an old plaster panel in the back room, had found a small, folded square of paper wedged behind a brick. He had handed it to the owners without opening it. The owners, sensibly, had handed it on to her.

The legal position is straightforward — the paper belongs to the owners of the flat — but the professional position is less so. A letter found behind plaster has, by the act of being placed there, a small claim on privacy. Whoever folded it that small, and pushed it that deep, intended that it not be read by the next people in the room.

The letter, when she opened it carefully under the lamp in her workshop, was in Slovak, dated 12 March 1947, in a careful, slightly old-fashioned hand. It was addressed to no one in particular. The opening line read simply: 'Whoever finds this, after I am gone.' The writer — a woman; the signature was 'Helena' — said she had lived in the flat since 1929, had been alone in it since her husband had not come back from the east in 1945, and was about to go away for a while, and might or might not return. The second paragraph was about the windowsill in the back room. She had grown geraniums on it for eighteen years. She asked that, if whoever lived there next was the kind of person who grew things, they might consider keeping a plant on that sill. She had a feeling, she wrote, that the room missed it when it was empty.

Vlasta sat with the letter for a long time. The standard move would have been to deposit a copy with the city archive, return the original to the owners, and let them decide whether to read it. What gave her pause was the second paragraph. The first paragraph was historical; the second was, in a quiet way, a request. The request had not been made to her. It had been made to whoever lived in that room next. And the owners, when she called them, told her they did not yet know whether they would keep the flat. They were considering selling.

In the end she did what she did. She made the archive copy. She returned the original to the owners in a sealed folder with a short letter of her own. In the letter she said, in the most neutral language she could manage, that the second paragraph was a request, and that the request, in her view, was addressed not strictly to them but to whoever, in this room, in the years to come, was the kind of person who grew things. She did not tell them what to do with it. She did, however, mention — because it was relevant — that geraniums prefer a bright, well-drained spot, and that the windowsill in question is south-facing.

grammarNotes.title: Subjunctive 'intended that ... not be read' + 'not strictly X but Y'
grammarNotes.bodyVi: Câu 'whoever folded it that small, and pushed it that deep, intended that it not be read by the next people in the room' dùng subjunctive trang trọng B2: 'intended that + subject + (should) not + V'. Cấu trúc 'addressed not strictly to them but to whoever ... was the kind of person who grew things' kết hợp 'not strictly X but Y' với mệnh đề quan hệ phổ quát 'whoever ... was' — đây là cấu trúc B2 cao dùng để chuyển trọng tâm khán giả của một lời thỉnh cầu.
grammarNotes.bodyEn: The clause 'whoever folded it that small, and pushed it that deep, intended that it not be read by the next people in the room' uses a formal B2 subjunctive: 'intended that + subject + (should) not + V'. The phrase 'addressed not strictly to them but to whoever ... was the kind of person who grew things' pairs 'not strictly X but Y' with a universal relative clause — an advanced B2 way of shifting the intended audience of a request.

translationVi:
Vlasta Dudášová được gọi đến căn hộ trên phố Heydukova vào một sáng thứ Ba tháng Mười Một. Chủ nhà đã sửa từ tháng Chín, và một thợ xây, khi đục bỏ một tấm vữa cũ ở phòng sau, tìm thấy một mảnh giấy gấp nhỏ kẹt phía sau một viên gạch. Anh đưa nó cho chủ nhà mà không mở. Chủ nhà, một cách phải lẽ, chuyển nó lại cho cô.

Trong những trường hợp như thế, người quyết định việc gì xảy ra tiếp là người bảo quản. Vị thế pháp lý thì rõ ràng — tờ giấy thuộc về chủ căn hộ — nhưng vị thế nghề thì kém rõ hơn. Một lá thư được tìm thấy sau lớp vữa, thông qua chính hành vi được đặt vào đó, có một quyền đòi quyền riêng tư nhỏ. Người gấp nó nhỏ đến vậy, và đẩy nó sâu đến vậy, đã chủ định rằng nó không được đọc bởi những người tiếp theo trong phòng.

Bức thư, khi cô mở ra cẩn thận dưới ngọn đèn trong xưởng, viết bằng tiếng Slovak, đề ngày 12 tháng Ba 1947, bằng nét chữ cẩn thận hơi cổ. Nó không gửi cho riêng ai. Câu mở đầu giản dị: 'Ai tìm thấy lá thư này, sau khi tôi đi rồi.' Thư dài hai đoạn. Người viết — một phụ nữ; chữ ký là 'Helena' — nói rằng bà đã sống trong căn hộ này từ 1929, rằng bà sống một mình ở đó từ khi chồng không trở về từ phía đông năm 1945, và rằng vào ngày viết bà sắp đi đâu đó một thời gian, có thể trở lại hoặc có thể không. Đoạn thứ hai nói về cái bệ cửa sổ ở phòng sau. Bà đã trồng phong lữ thảo trên đó suốt mười tám năm. Bà xin rằng, nếu người sống ở đây tiếp theo là kiểu người trồng trọt, họ hãy cân nhắc giữ một chậu cây trên cái bệ ấy. Bà có cảm giác, bà viết, rằng căn phòng nhớ nó khi nó trống.

Vlasta ngồi với lá thư một lúc lâu trước khi quyết định bất cứ điều gì. Cách làm nghề chuẩn lẽ ra là gửi một bản sao tại lưu trữ thành phố, trả bản gốc cho chủ nhà, và khuyến nghị chủ nhà tự quyết định có đọc nó không. Điều khiến cô ngập ngừng là đoạn thứ hai. Đoạn đầu là lịch sử; đoạn hai, theo một cách khẽ khàng, là một lời thỉnh cầu. Lời thỉnh cầu đó không gửi cho cô. Nó gửi cho người sống ở đó tiếp theo. Và chủ nhà, khi cô gọi, nói họ chưa biết có giữ căn hộ không. Họ đang cân nhắc bán.

Cuối cùng cô đã làm điều cô đã làm. Cô tạo bản lưu trữ. Cô trả bản gốc cho chủ nhà trong một cặp niêm phong cùng một lá thư ngắn của chính cô. Trong thư, cô giải thích nội dung cặp. Cô cũng nói, bằng thứ ngôn ngữ trung tính nhất có thể, rằng đoạn thứ hai là một lời thỉnh cầu, và lời ấy, theo cô, không phải gửi đúng cho họ mà cho bất kỳ ai, trong căn phòng này, trong những năm sắp tới, là kiểu người trồng trọt. Cô không bảo họ phải làm gì với nó. Tuy nhiên cô có nhắc — vì điều đó có liên quan — rằng phong lữ thảo thích vị trí sáng, thoát nước tốt, và bệ cửa sổ đang nói tới là hướng nam.

questions.prompt: Where was the letter found and by whom?
options: Wedged behind a brick in a back-room wall on Heydukova Street, by a builder during renovation; handed unopened to the owners.
Inside a book in a library.

questions.prompt: Why does Vlasta say the legal position and the professional position differ?
options: Legally the paper belongs to the owners, but professionally a letter folded that small and pushed that deep has, by the act of being placed there, a small claim on privacy.
Legally it is a state secret.
Professionally it must always be published.

questions.prompt: What did the person who hid the letter intend?
options: That it not be read by the next people in the room.

questions.prompt: What language was the letter in, and when was it written?
options: Slovak, 12 March 1947, in a careful slightly old-fashioned hand.

questions.prompt: To whom was the letter addressed?
options: No one in particular — 'Whoever finds this, after I am gone.'

questions.prompt: What does Helena say about her own circumstances?
options: She had lived in the flat since 1929, had been alone there since her husband had not come back from the east in 1945, and might or might not return from the trip she was about to take.

questions.prompt: What is the second paragraph of Helena's letter about?
options: The windowsill in the back room, where she had grown geraniums for eighteen years, and a request to the next person.
That if whoever lived in the flat next was the kind of person who grew things, they might consider keeping a plant on the sill.
That the new resident send her a letter back.
That the flat be turned into a museum.

questions.prompt: What does Helena say she 'has a feeling' about?
options: That the room missed the plant when the sill was empty.
That the flat would be sold.

questions.prompt: What would the standard professional move have been?
options: Deposit a copy with the city archive, return the original to the owners, and recommend that they decide for themselves whether to read it.
Keep the original in the workshop.

questions.prompt: What gave Vlasta pause about the standard procedure?
options: The second paragraph was a request — and the request had not been made to her, but to whoever lived in the room next.
The owners refused to take it.

questions.prompt: What complication did the owners introduce?
options: They did not yet know whether they would keep the flat — they were considering selling.

questions.prompt: What did Vlasta finally do with the original?
options: Returned it to the owners in a sealed folder, with her own short covering letter explaining what was inside.
Kept it in her workshop indefinitely.
Sent it to the city archive only.

questions.prompt: In her own covering letter, how does Vlasta describe the intended audience of Helena's request?
options: Addressed not strictly to the current owners but to whoever, in this room, in the years to come, was the kind of person who grew things.
Addressed only to the current owners.

questions.prompt: What practical detail does Vlasta add at the end of her own letter, 'because it was relevant'?
options: That geraniums prefer a bright, well-drained spot, and the windowsill in question is south-facing.
That the city archive will pay for the letter.
That the builder should be paid extra.

questions.prompt: Which of these is the passage NOT claiming?
options: That the legal owner of a found letter is automatically also its only intended reader.
That conservators routinely face decisions where law and professional ethics diverge.
That Vlasta deliberately stopped short of telling the owners what to do.
That Helena framed her request as a possible future kindness, not a demand.
questions.explanation: The passage carefully distinguishes legal owner from intended reader.
questions.hint: Look for the option contradicted by the legal-vs-professional distinction.

questions.prompt: What is the tone of the piece?
options: Restrained, considered, and quietly principled — without sentimentality.
questions.explanation: Vlasta's manner is described and modelled by the prose itself.

options: A letter hidden in a wall can carry a request whose intended audience is the future occupant, not the present owner; a conservator's duty may be to keep that distinction alive without imposing it.
Old letters should always be archived.

blanks.explanation: Whoever lived in that room next.
To whoever was the kind of person.

criticalThinkingQuestion: Vlasta could have simply returned the letter and stopped there. Instead she added a covering note pointing out that Helena's request was addressed not strictly to the current owners but to whoever next loved the room — and then quietly mentioned that the sill is south-facing. Was she overstepping her professional role, or doing it more deeply? Is there a difference, in any work you know, between 'returning the object' and 'keeping the request alive'?
